message.content:
Below is a catalog of **concrete checks** an automated launch auditor can run against a live URL, split into **Front‚ÄëEnd** and **Infrastructure** reliability/performance concerns.

For each check:

- **Name**
- **What it verifies**
- **Subcategory**
- **Standard ref (if any)**
- **Authoritative source**

I only cite authoritative sources such as **web.dev, MDN, Chrome/Lighthouse docs, RFCs, IETF, Google SRE/Cloud docs**.

---

## A. Core Web Vitals & Other UX Timing Metrics

### 1. Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) Threshold

- **Check name**: LCP threshold compliance  
- **Verifies**: The page‚Äôs **Largest Contentful Paint** occurs within **‚â§2.5‚ÄØs (good)** and flags if between **2.5‚Äì4‚ÄØs (needs improvement)** or **>4‚ÄØs (poor)**.[1][2]  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí User experience ‚Üí Core Web Vitals  
- **Standard ref**: Core Web Vitals thresholds: Good/NI/Poor.[1][2]  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúLargest Contentful Paint (LCP)‚Äù and CWV overview.[1][2]

### 2. Interaction to Next Paint (INP) Threshold

- **Check name**: INP threshold compliance  
- **Verifies**: Page‚Äôs **Interaction to Next Paint** is **‚â§200‚ÄØms (good)**, 200‚Äì500‚ÄØms (needs improvement), or **>500‚ÄØms (poor)**.[3]  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí User experience ‚Üí Core Web Vitals  
- **Standard ref**: Core Web Vitals INP thresholds.[3]  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúInteraction to Next Paint (INP)‚Äù and CWV docs.[3]

### 3. Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) Threshold

- **Check name**: CLS threshold compliance  
- **Verifies**: Page‚Äôs **Cumulative Layout Shift score** is **‚â§0.1 (good)**, 0.1‚Äì0.25 (needs improvement), **>0.25 (poor)**.[4]  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Visual stability ‚Üí Core Web Vitals  
- **Standard ref**: Core Web Vitals CLS thresholds.[4]  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúCumulative Layout Shift (CLS)‚Äù.[4]

### 4. First Contentful Paint (FCP)

- **Check name**: FCP performance classification  
- **Verifies**: **First Contentful Paint** timing against Lighthouse‚Äôs performance scoring ranges (good/needs improvement/poor).[5][6]  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Loading performance  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse performance metrics (FCP).[5][6]  
- **Authoritative source**: Lighthouse performance scoring docs on web.dev.[5][6]

### 5. Time to First Byte (TTFB)

- **Check name**: TTFB budget compliance  
- **Verifies**: **Time To First Byte** is within recommended budgets (e.g. Lighthouse treats **server response time** as an audit; TTFB is widely used).[7][8]  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Network latency / Backend responsiveness  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúReduce initial server response time‚Äù audit uses TTFB.[7]  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúTime to First Byte (TTFB)‚Äù and Lighthouse ‚ÄúReduce initial server response time‚Äù.[7][8]

---

## B. Lighthouse Performance Audits (Page‚ÄëLevel)

The auditor can run Lighthouse in CI and evaluate individual audits.

### 6. Overall Lighthouse Performance Score

- **Check name**: Lighthouse performance score threshold  
- **Verifies**: Page‚Äôs **Performance score** is above a configured threshold (e.g. ‚â•90/100 for launch). Metrics include FCP, LCP, CLS, INP, Speed Index, TBT.[5][6]  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Aggregate performance  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse performance scoring.[5][6]  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúHow performance scores are calculated‚Äù.[5][6]

### 7. Eliminate Render‚ÄëBlocking Resources

- **Check name**: Render‚Äëblocking resources audit  
- **Verifies**: Presence of **CSS/JS in the critical path** that blocks first paint; flags large or non‚Äëcritical resources blocking rendering.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Loading optimization  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúEliminate render‚Äëblocking resources‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúEliminate render‚Äëblocking resources‚Äù.

### 8. Minify CSS

- **Check name**: CSS minification audit  
- **Verifies**: CSS response bodies are **minified** (no unnecessary whitespace/comments) to reduce transfer size.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Asset optimization  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúMinify CSS‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúMinify CSS‚Äù.

### 9. Minify JavaScript

- **Check name**: JavaScript minification audit  
- **Verifies**: JS bundles are **minified** to reduce transfer size and parse/compile time.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Asset optimization  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúMinify JavaScript‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúMinify JavaScript‚Äù.

### 10. Remove Unused CSS

- **Check name**: Unused CSS coverage  
- **Verifies**: Amount of **unused CSS** in loaded stylesheets and flags excessive unused rules.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Asset optimization ‚Üí Code elimination  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúReduce unused CSS‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúReduce unused CSS‚Äù.

### 11. Remove Unused JavaScript

- **Check name**: Unused JavaScript coverage  
- **Verifies**: Amount of **unused JS** bytes and suggests code‚Äësplitting/treeshaking.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Asset optimization ‚Üí Code elimination  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúReduce unused JavaScript‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúReduce unused JavaScript‚Äù.

### 12. Reduce JavaScript Execution Time / Main‚ÄëThread Work

- **Check name**: Main‚Äëthread work budget  
- **Verifies**: Time spent doing JS execution, style/layout, and other main‚Äëthread tasks stays within budgets.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Runtime performance  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúMinimize main‚Äëthread work‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúMinimize main‚Äëthread work‚Äù.

### 13. Avoid Long Tasks

- **Check name**: Long task detection  
- **Verifies**: Presence of **long tasks** (>50‚ÄØms) that block the main thread and harm INP/TBT.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Runtime responsiveness  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúMinimize main‚Äëthread work‚Äù / ‚ÄúAvoid long main‚Äëthread tasks‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev main‚Äëthread guidance.

### 14. Efficient Text Compression (Gzip/Brotli)

- **Check name**: Text compression enabled  
- **Verifies**: Transfer of compressible resources (HTML, CSS, JS, JSON, etc.) with **gzip or brotli** via `Content-Encoding`.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí HTTP optimization ‚Üí Compression  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúEnable text compression‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúEnable text compression‚Äù.

### 15. Serve Images in Next‚ÄëGen Formats

- **Check name**: Modern image formats (WebP/AVIF)  
- **Verifies**: Images are served in **modern formats** such as WebP or AVIF where browser support exists, or flags legacy formats (JPEG/PNG) that could be smaller.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Media optimization  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúServe images in next-gen formats‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúServe images in next-gen formats‚Äù.

### 16. Properly Size Images

- **Check name**: Image dimensions and responsive sizing  
- **Verifies**: Images are not delivered at resolutions significantly larger than rendered size; checks `srcset`/sizes usage.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Media optimization  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúProperly size images‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúProperly size images‚Äù.

### 17. Efficient Image Encoding

- **Check name**: Optimized image encoding  
- **Verifies**: Images are efficiently compressed (quality/size) and flags images that can be significantly reduced without quality loss.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Media optimization  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúEfficiently encode images‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúEfficiently encode images‚Äù.

### 18. Defer Offscreen Images / Lazy‚ÄëLoading

- **Check name**: Offscreen image lazy‚Äëloading  
- **Verifies**: Non‚Äëcritical (below‚Äëthe‚Äëfold) images are **lazy‚Äëloaded**, e.g. `loading="lazy"`, to reduce initial bytes.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Media optimization ‚Üí Loading strategy  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúDefer offscreen images‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúDefer offscreen images‚Äù.

### 19. Preload Key Requests

- **Check name**: Preload critical resources  
- **Verifies**: Important resources used early (e.g. hero image, above‚Äëthe‚Äëfold CSS) are preloaded via `<link rel="preload">` where appropriate.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Resource prioritization  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúPreload key requests‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúPreload key requests‚Äù.

### 20. Avoid Large Network Payloads / Page Weight

- **Check name**: Page‚Äëweight / payload budget  
- **Verifies**: Total bytes transferred are under a configured **performance budget**; Lighthouse flags pages > ~4‚ÄØMB by default.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Page weight / performance budgets  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúAvoid enormous network payloads‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúAvoid enormous network payloads‚Äù.

### 21. Set and Enforce Performance Budgets

- **Check name**: Performance budget compliance  
- **Verifies**: Custom budgets for metrics (LCP, FCP, TBT, total JS size, image bytes, etc.) using Lighthouse Budgets or similar tooling.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Performance governance  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúMeasure performance with the Performance panel and Lighthouse‚Äù and budgets feature.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúPerformance budgets‚Äù.

### 22. Lazy‚ÄëLoad Non‚ÄëCritical Third‚ÄëParty Scripts

- **Check name**: Third‚Äëparty script loading strategy  
- **Verifies**: Third‚Äëparty scripts (analytics, ads, widgets) are deferred, async, or loaded after critical rendering; checks their impact in Lighthouse‚Äôs **Third‚Äëparty usage** audit.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Third‚Äëparty impact  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúReduce the impact of third-party code‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúReduce the impact of third‚Äëparty code‚Äù.

### 23. Reduce JavaScript Bundle Size

- **Check name**: JavaScript bundle size budgets  
- **Verifies**: Total JS size transferred stays under defined budgets; uses Lighthouse and network logs.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Asset size budgets  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse budgets and ‚ÄúAvoid enormous network payloads‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev on budgets and payloads.

---

## C. HTTP Protocol, Transport & Connection Behavior

### 24. HTTP/2 Support

- **Check name**: HTTP/2 availability  
- **Verifies**: Whether the origin and/or CDN serves the page and static assets over **HTTP/2** (`h2` in ALPN / protocol).  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Protocol support  
- **Standard ref**: IETF **RFC 7540** (HTTP/2).  
- **Authoritative source**: RFC¬Ý7540 (HTTP/2).  

### 25. HTTP/3 (QUIC) Support

- **Check name**: HTTP/3 availability  
- **Verifies**: Whether server/CDN supports **HTTP/3** via **QUIC** (ALPN `h3`).  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Protocol support  
- **Standard ref**: IETF HTTP/3 (RFC¬Ý9114) and QUIC (RFC¬Ý9000) specs.  
- **Authoritative source**: IETF HTTP/3 RFCs.

### 26. HTTP Keep‚ÄëAlive and Connection Reuse

- **Check name**: Connection reuse / keep‚Äëalive  
- **Verifies**: Use of persistent connections (`Connection: keep-alive`) and reuse across requests; reduced TCP/TLS handshakes.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Transport efficiency  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP/1.1 persistence in RFC¬Ý7230 (obsoleted by RFC¬Ý9110 but semantics similar).  
- **Authoritative source**: HTTP/1.1 message syntax and routing spec.

### 27. TLS Version and Configuration

- **Check name**: Modern TLS usage  
- **Verifies**: Use of **TLS 1.2+** and strong cipher suites for HTTPS endpoints.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Transport security/performance  
- **Standard ref**: IETF TLS 1.2 (RFC¬Ý5246) / TLS 1.3 (RFC¬Ý8446).  
- **Authoritative source**: IETF TLS RFCs; Chrome/MDN recommendations for TLS versions.

---

## D. Caching, CDN, and Edge Behavior

### 28. Cache-Control Headers

- **Check name**: Cache‚ÄëControl policy audit  
- **Verifies**: Presence and correctness of `Cache-Control` directives (e.g. `max-age`, `s-maxage`, `public`, `private`, `no-store`) for HTML and static assets.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí HTTP caching  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP caching semantics in RFC¬Ý7234 / updated in RFC¬Ý9111.  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN ‚ÄúCache-Control‚Äù and HTTP caching docs.

### 29. ETag Presence and Correctness

- **Check name**: ETag conditional caching support  
- **Verifies**: Resources include strong or weak **ETag** headers and that server responds with `304 Not Modified` on `If-None-Match` when appropriate.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí HTTP caching  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP conditional requests and ETag semantics in RFC¬Ý7232 / RFC¬Ý9110.  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN ‚ÄúETag‚Äù and HTTP caching.

### 30. Last-Modified Header Presence

- **Check name**: Last‚ÄëModified / If‚ÄëModified‚ÄëSince support  
- **Verifies**: Resources set `Last-Modified` and server correctly handles `If-Modified-Since` requests.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí HTTP caching  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP conditional requests in RFC¬Ý7232 / RFC¬Ý9110.  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN ‚ÄúLast-Modified‚Äù.

### 31. CDN Presence and Edge Caching

- **Check name**: CDN usage & edge cache effectiveness  
- **Verifies**: Responses are served via a **CDN** (via DNS CNAMEs, headers) and static assets are cached at edge locations with appropriate cache headers and `s-maxage` if using shared caches.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Content delivery  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP caching semantics (shared caches) RFC¬Ý9111.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúContent delivery networks‚Äù and HTTP caching docs.

### 32. Vary and Content Negotiation

- **Check name**: Correct `Vary` headers for content negotiation  
- **Verifies**: When using compression or language negotiation, responses set `Vary: Accept-Encoding`, `Vary: Accept-Language`, etc., to keep caches correct.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí HTTP caching correctness  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP caching / Vary header semantics in RFC¬Ý7231 / 7234 / 9110.  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN ‚ÄúVary‚Äù.

---

## E. Image Optimization & Media Delivery

(Several covered under Lighthouse; these are the concrete checks you‚Äôd implement.)

- **Serve modern formats** (WebP/AVIF) ‚Üí see check 15.  
- **Proper sizing** ‚Üí check 16.  
- **Efficient encoding** ‚Üí check 17.  
- **Lazy‚Äëloading** ‚Üí check 18.  

Additional infra‚Äëoriented media checks:

### 33. Accept-Encoding Negotiation for Images

- **Check name**: Image compression / `Accept-Encoding` handling  
- **Verifies**: Server honors `Accept-Encoding` to serve compressed image payloads where appropriate (e.g., WebP with gzip).  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí HTTP negotiation / compression  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP content negotiation; `Accept` and `Accept-Encoding` headers in RFC¬Ý9110.  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN on `Accept`, `Accept-Encoding` and HTTP content negotiation.

---

## F. Third‚ÄëParty Scripts & External Resources

### 34. Third‚ÄëParty Script Timing & Size

- **Check name**: Third‚Äëparty script cost  
- **Verifies**: Total bytes and execution time contributed by **third‚Äëparty** origins; identifies slow or heavy tags.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Third‚Äëparty performance impact  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse ‚ÄúReduce the impact of third-party code‚Äù.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev third‚Äëparty performance guide.

### 35. Third‚ÄëParty Blocking of Main Thread

- **Check name**: Main‚Äëthread blocking due to third‚Äëparty  
- **Verifies**: Whether third‚Äëparty JS creates **long tasks** or blocks interaction, as surfaced in Lighthouse and Performance panel.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Third‚Äëparty impact ‚Üí Responsiveness  
- **Standard ref**: Same as above; CWV/INP guidance.[3]  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev INP and third‚Äëparty impact docs.[3]

### 36. Async/Defer Usage for Third‚ÄëParty Scripts

- **Check name**: Non‚Äëblocking third‚Äëparty loading  
- **Verifies**: `<script>` tags for third‚Äëparty are marked `async` or `defer` (or loaded via worker) to avoid blocking parsing.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Third‚Äëparty loading strategy  
- **Standard ref**: Lighthouse guidance on blocking scripts.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúOptimize third-party JavaScript‚Äù.

---

## G. Uptime, Availability & Health Monitoring

### 37. HTTP Uptime / Availability Monitoring

- **Check name**: 24/7 uptime / SLA monitoring  
- **Verifies**: Periodic checks (synthetic) that the URL returns successful HTTP status codes (2xx/3xx) with expected content; tracks SLO/SLA error budget.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Reliability monitoring  
- **Standard ref**: Google SRE‚Äôs SLI/SLO/SLA and error‚Äëbudget model.  
- **Authoritative source**: Google SRE books and SRE workbook (availability monitoring).

### 38. Health‚ÄëCheck Endpoints

- **Check name**: Health probe endpoint behavior  
- **Verifies**: Dedicated **health check endpoints** (e.g. `/healthz`, `/readyz`) exist and return simple 200 OK when service is healthy; used by load balancers/orchestrators.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Health checks / readiness & liveness  
- **Standard ref**: Kubernetes / Google Cloud guidance on liveness/readiness probes.  
- **Authoritative source**: Google Cloud / Kubernetes health check documentation.

### 39. Graceful Handling of 5xx Errors

- **Check name**: Friendly and cache‚Äësafe 5xx responses  
- **Verifies**: On **5xx** errors, app serves a **graceful error page**, does not expose stack traces or sensitive data, and sets appropriate cache headers to avoid caching transient errors.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Failure handling  
- **Standard ref**: Google SRE guidance on graceful degradation and error handling; HTTP semantics for 5xx.  
- **Authoritative source**: Google SRE book; MDN HTTP status codes.

### 40. Retry-After on 429/503

- **Check name**: `Retry-After` header compliance  
- **Verifies**: When returning **429 Too Many Requests** or **503 Service Unavailable**, server includes a `Retry-After` header with either a HTTP date or delay in seconds to guide clients and not overload the system.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Rate limiting / overload protection  
- **Standard ref**: `Retry-After` header semantics in HTTP spec (RFC¬Ý7231 / RFC¬Ý9110).  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN ‚ÄúRetry-After‚Äù.

### 41. Graceful Degradation Under Load

- **Check name**: Overload behavior / partial availability  
- **Verifies**: Under high load, system prefers **partial functionality** or degraded UX over total failure, with appropriate status codes and messaging; can be probed via synthetic load tests.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Resilience patterns  
- **Standard ref**: Google SRE guidance on load shedding, graceful degradation.  
- **Authoritative source**: Google SRE book / Reliable Web App pattern.[3]

---

## H. Application‚ÄëLevel Reliability Checks

### 42. Idempotent Safe Methods for Retries

- **Check name**: Safe retry semantics on GET/HEAD  
- **Verifies**: Idempotent methods (GET, HEAD) can be retried by clients/monitors without side‚Äëeffects; important for network retries.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí HTTP semantics / robustness  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP method semantics, safe/idempotent methods in RFC¬Ý9110.  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN ‚ÄúHTTP request methods‚Äù.

### 43. Error Response Format Consistency

- **Check name**: Consistent error schema  
- **Verifies**: JSON/HTML error responses are consistent (fields, codes) to support client‚Äëside reliability handling; checked by synthetic probing of known error conditions.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd & API ‚Üí Error handling  
- **Standard ref**: Not formalized in a single RFC; aligns with Google API design practices.  
- **Authoritative source**: Google API design guide; SRE on robust error handling.

---

## I. Miscellaneous Performance & Reliability Checks

### 44. Use of Service Workers for Offline / Resilience (if applicable)

- **Check name**: Service worker registration and strategy  
- **Verifies**: Presence of a **service worker** that caches static assets and possibly provides offline fallback; ensures it does not harm freshness or correctness.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Offline / resilience  
- **Standard ref**: Service Worker spec; web.dev offline practices.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúThe Service Worker Lifecycle‚Äù and ‚ÄúOffline and background‚Äù.

### 45. Resource Hints (preconnect, dns-prefetch)

- **Check name**: Preconnect / DNS Prefetch usage  
- **Verifies**: Use of `<link rel="preconnect">` and `dns-prefetch` for critical cross‚Äëorigin resources (e.g., CDN, APIs), minimizing connection setup time.  
- **Subcategory**: Front‚ÄëEnd ‚Üí Network optimization  
- **Standard ref**: Resource Hints spec; Lighthouse guidance on preconnect.  
- **Authoritative source**: web.dev ‚ÄúPreconnect to required origins‚Äù.

### 46. HTTP Response Compression for HTML/CSS/JS/JSON

- **Check name**: Compression coverage across resource types  
- **Verifies**: All text‚Äëbased resources are served with `Content-Encoding: gzip` or `br` when client advertises support; cross‚Äëchecks with `Accept-Encoding`.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Compression  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP compression / content codings in RFC¬Ý9110.  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN ‚ÄúAccept-Encoding‚Äù, ‚ÄúContent-Encoding‚Äù; web.dev ‚ÄúEnable text compression‚Äù.

### 47. Response Chunking / Streaming (for large payloads)

- **Check name**: Streaming for large responses  
- **Verifies**: For large HTML/JSON responses, server uses **chunked transfer encoding** or streaming to reduce time‚Äëto‚Äëfirst‚Äëbyte and allow progressive rendering.  
- **Subcategory**: Infrastructure ‚Üí Transfer optimization  
- **Standard ref**: HTTP/1.1 chunked encoding in RFC¬Ý7230 / RFC¬Ý9112.  
- **Authoritative source**: MDN ‚ÄúTransfer-Encoding‚Äù.

---

## J. How to Integrate These in an Automated Launch Auditor

To make this practical in CI/CD:

- Use **Lighthouse CI** (or PageSpeed Insights API) to programmatically run checks 1‚Äì23 with thresholds.  
- Use **custom HTTP probe scripts** (Node, Go) with **DevTools Protocol** / `fetch` to validate HTTP headers and protocols: checks 24‚Äì32, 40, 46.  
- Use **synthetic uptime monitors** (Google Cloud Monitoring, etc.) to run checks 37‚Äì41.  
- Apply **performance budgets** via Lighthouse Budgets JSON for checks 20‚Äì23, 33, 47.

If you‚Äôd like, I can turn this into a machine‚Äëreadable checklist (e.g., JSON or table) suitable for wiring directly into your auditor.
